content.text: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 is the international standard for Artificial Intelligence Management Systems (AIMS). Published in December 2023 by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC), it is the world's first auditable management system standard specifically for AI. Unlike the EU AI Act, which is a legal regulation, ISO/IEC 42001 is a voluntary standard that organisations can be certified against by an accredited third-party auditor.

The standard follows the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle, the same high-level structure used by ISO 9001 (quality management) and ISO 27001 (information security). For developers of agentic AI systems, this means the engineering work of building and monitoring agents needs to be governed by a documented, auditable management system — not just good engineering practices.

attrs.alt: ISO/IEC 42001 — AI Management System PDCA Cycle

content.text: Why ISO/IEC 42001 Matters for Agentic AI

An agentic AI system that reads files, calls APIs, and takes actions in the world is exactly the kind of high-impact technology that certification bodies are scrutinising. Certification to ISO/IEC 42001 demonstrates to regulators, enterprise customers, and auditors that your organisation has systematic governance in place — not just ad-hoc risk review.
Enterprise procurement: many enterprise contracts now require AI management system certification as a vendor qualification
EU AI Act alignment: ISO/IEC 42001 is cited in the EU AI Act's standardisation roadmap — certified organisations gain a strong presumption of conformity
Third-party trust: certification lets customers verify your AI governance claims without performing their own audits
Continuous monitoring: the standard requires ongoing performance evaluation, matching what agentic systems need anyway
ISO/IEC 42001 has 10 numbered clauses. Clauses 1–3 cover scope, references, and definitions. Clauses 4–10 contain the actual requirements and map to the PDCA cycle.
Understand internal/external factors; identify interested parties; define AIMS scope
Top management must demonstrate commitment; establish AI policy; assign roles
Address risks and opportunities; set AI objectives; plan AI impact assessments
Provide resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information
AI system lifecycle (plan, design, develop, deploy, monitor, retire)
Monitor, measure, analyse, evaluate; internal audit; management review
Nonconformity and corrective action; continual improvement

Before building any management system, the organisation must understand the context in which it operates. For an agentic AI provider, this means identifying which AI systems are in scope, who the interested parties are, and what regulatory, contractual, and ethical obligations apply.

Top management must provide evidence of their commitment to the AIMS — not a one-time endorsement but active, ongoing involvement. The AI policy is the anchor document: it states the organisation's intent and commitment and is the basis against which internal audits assess conformity.

from datetime import date

@dataclass
class AIPolicy:
    """
    Clause 5.2 — AI Policy (documented information, must be maintained and communicated).
    This is not a code artefact but it MUST exist as a maintained document.
    This dataclass represents the structured content of that policy.
    """
    organisation_name: str
    policy_version: str
    approved_by: str       # title of top management role (e.g. "CTO")
    approved_date: date

    # Mandatory content per Clause 5.2
    commitment_to_responsible_ai: str
    commitment_to_human_rights: str
    commitment_to_continual_improvement: str

    ai_objectives_reference: str       # pointer to the objectives document (Clause 6.2)
    scope_reference: str               # pointer to AIMS scope document (Clause 4.3)

    review_cadence_months: int = 12

    def is_current(self, as_of: date) -> bool:
        months_since = (as_of.year - self.approved_date.year) * 12 + (
            as_of.month - self.approved_date.month
        )
        return months_since <= self.review_cadence_months

@dataclass
class AIRole:
    """Clause 5.3 — Roles, responsibilities, and authorities."""
    role_title: str
    responsibilities: list[str]
    authority: list[str]
    reports_to: str

Annex A of ISO/IEC 42001 defines reference controls, including A.6 which mandates AI impact assessments (AI-IA). An AI impact assessment evaluates potential harms to individuals, groups, and society from the AI system before and during deployment.
An AI impact assessment is not a one-time gate. ISO/IEC 42001 requires it to be performed at planning time and updated whenever there is a material change to the system or its deployment context.

Clause 8 is where engineering work meets the management system. It requires the organisation to plan, design, develop, deploy, monitor, and retire AI systems in accordance with the AIMS. Each phase must produce documented evidence.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class AISystemDesignRecord:
    """
    Clause 8.3 / Annex A A.4 — Design and development documented information.
    Evidence required: architecture decision, data requirements, evaluation criteria.
    """
    system_id: str
    design_version: str
    architecture_description: str
    intended_inputs: list[str]
    intended_outputs: list[str]
    training_data_sources: list[str]
    evaluation_criteria: dict[str, float]   # metric -> threshold, e.g. {"accuracy": 0.90}
    human_oversight_mechanism: str
    fallback_behaviour: str           # what the system does if it fails or is uncertain
    author: str
    reviewed_by: str
    review_date: datetime

@dataclass
class AISystemDeploymentRecord:
    """Clause 8.4 — Deployment documented information."""
    system_id: str
    deployment_environment: str
    deployment_date: datetime
    impact_assessment_ref: str        # reference to completed AIImpactAssessment
    design_record_ref: str
    acceptance_test_results: dict[str, bool]   # test_name -> passed
    deployer: str

    def all_tests_passed(self) -> bool:
        return all(self.acceptance_test_results.values())

Clause 8.5 and Annex A A.9 cover ongoing monitoring of deployed AI systems. For agentic systems this maps directly to production observability — logging inputs, outputs, tool calls, and deviations from expected behaviour.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class AIPerformanceRecord:
    """Clause 8.5 / A.9 — Ongoing monitoring record."""
    system_id: str
    period_start: datetime
    period_end: datetime
    metric_name: str
    observed_value: float
    target_value: float
    within_threshold: bool
    sample_size: int
    notes: str = ""

class AIMonitoringDashboard:
    """Aggregate performance records for management review (Clause 9.3)."""

    def __init__(self, system_id: str):
        self.system_id = system_id
        self._records: list[AIPerformanceRecord] = []

    def add_record(self, r: AIPerformanceRecord) -> None:
        self._records.append(r)

    def out_of_threshold(self) -> list[AIPerformanceRecord]:
        return [r for r in self._records if not r.within_threshold]

    def management_review_summary(self) -> dict:
        recent = sorted(self._records, key=lambda r: r.period_end, reverse=True)[:10]
        return {
            "system_id": self.system_id,
            "records_reviewed": len(recent),
            "out_of_threshold": len([r for r in recent if not r.within_threshold]),
            "action_required": any(not r.within_threshold for r in recent),
        }
Clause 9 requires the organisation to monitor, measure, analyse, and evaluate AIMS performance. It has three components: ongoing monitoring, internal audit, and management review.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class InternalAuditReport:
    """Clause 9.2 — Internal audit documented information."""
    audit_id: str
    audit_date: date
    auditor: str                      # must be independent of the audited activity
    scope: list[str]                  # which clauses and systems were audited
    criteria: list[str]               # standards, policies, objectives used as basis
    findings: list[str]               # what was observed
    nonconformities: list[str]        # deviations from requirements
    observations: list[str]           # areas for potential improvement (not yet NC)
    conclusion: str                   # overall AIMS conformity conclusion

@dataclass
class ManagementReviewRecord:
    """Clause 9.3 — Management review documented information."""
    review_date: date
    attendees: list[str]
    inputs_reviewed: list[str]        # audit reports, performance data, complaints
    decisions: list[str]              # resource allocations, policy changes
    action_items: list[tuple[str, str, date]]   # (action, owner, due_date)
    ai_objectives_status: dict[str, str]         # objective -> "on track" / "at risk" / "achieved"

Annex A of ISO/IEC 42001 provides reference controls — specific practices that support AIMS requirements. Organisations select which controls to implement based on their risk assessment; any excluded control must be justified in a Statement of Applicability (SoA).
Responsible use policy, prohibited use list
For agentic AI, A.5 (impact assessment), A.6 (lifecycle controls), A.7 (data governance), and A.9 (human oversight) are the controls that require the most engineering work to implement.
Relationship to Other Standards and Regulations
ISO/IEC 42001 is designed to be compatible with other ISO management system standards and to complement sector-specific AI regulations.
Compatible structure; AI security controls can be integrated into existing 27001 ISMS
PDCA structure is identical; AI quality controls extend existing QMS
42001 certification cited in standardisation roadmap; gives presumption of conformity for process requirements
Complementary frameworks; MAP/MEASURE/MANAGE map to Clause 6/8/9 respectively
42001's data governance controls (A.7) support but do not replace GDPR compliance

The most common path for organisations with existing ISO 27001 certification is to extend their existing management system to cover AI-specific requirements, rather than building a separate AIMS from scratch. This leverages existing audit processes, document control, and corrective action procedures.
Certification to ISO/IEC 42001 follows the standard two-stage audit process used by all ISO management system standards:
Stage 1 (document review): the auditor reviews AIMS documentation — AI policy, scope, risk assessments, impact assessments, objectives. Typically 0.5–1 day remote.

Stage 2 (conformity assessment): the auditor visits operations, interviews staff, reviews evidence of implementation (monitoring records, audit reports, management review minutes). Typically 1–3 days on-site or remote.
Certification issued: if Stage 2 finds no major nonconformities. Minor nonconformities require a correction plan but do not block certification.
Surveillance audits: annual audits verify the AIMS remains effective. Full recertification every 3 years.
Use this ordered list to scope an ISO/IEC 42001 implementation project for an organisation building agentic AI systems:
Clause 4: Document AIMS scope; identify interested parties and applicable regulations
Clause 5: Draft AI policy; assign AI management roles (AI system owner, AIMS coordinator)
Clause 6: Identify AI risks and opportunities; set measurable AI objectives; schedule impact assessments for each system
Annex A.5/A.6: Complete AI impact assessment for highest-risk system; document design and deployment records
Annex A.7: Establish data governance procedure covering training data provenance and quality
Annex A.9: Document human oversight requirements; build monitoring dashboard (Clause 9.1)
Clause 9.2: Plan and execute first internal audit (can use a consultant for the first cycle)
Clause 9.3: Conduct management review; document decisions and action items
Clause 10: Establish nonconformity and corrective action log
External audit: engage accredited certification body for Stage 1 and Stage 2 audits

ISO/IEC 42001 is the international AI management system standard that organisations can be certified against to demonstrate systematic AI governance. It applies the PDCA cycle to AI — Plan (context, policy, objectives), Do (operation and lifecycle), Check (monitoring, internal audit), Act (improvement and corrective action). Annex A provides reference controls covering impact assessment, AI lifecycle, data governance, transparency, and human oversight. For agentic AI developers, the standard's most operationally relevant requirements are the AI impact assessment (Annex A.5), lifecycle documentation (Annex A.6), and ongoing monitoring (Clause 9.1). Certification is evidence-based and auditable by an accredited third party, making it the credibility benchmark that enterprise customers and regulators increasingly require.
